channel.title: Latest from Android Central

item.title: Google's latest Android 17 beta fixes Bluetooth headaches and Gemini crashes
item.description: Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 is here, fixing Bluetooth, Gemini, and touch bugs on Pixels, though Pixel 6 users are officially reaching the end of the beta road.
item.author: techkritiko@gmail.com (Jay Bonggolto)
item.dc:creator: Jay Bonggolto
item.dc:description: <p>Jay Bonggolto always keeps a nose for news. He has been writing about consumer tech and apps for as long as he can remember, and he has used a variety of Android phones since falling in love with Jelly Bean. When he's not writing, he likes to spend time outside, stealing scenes with his phone camera. Send him a direct message via X or LinkedIn.</p>

media:content.media:description: Android 17 Easter egg on Pixel 10, 10 Pro XL and Pixel 10 Pro Fold
media:content.media:text: Android 17 Easter egg on Pixel 10, 10 Pro XL and Pixel 10 Pro Fold
media:content.media:title: Android 17 Easter egg on Pixel 10, 10 Pro XL and Pixel 10 Pro Fold

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 is officially here, kicking off testing for Google's next Pixel Feature Drop arriving in December 2026.
  • This beta includes fixes for Bluetooth reconnection failures, lock screen media glitches, Gemini-related reboot crashes, and touch input bugs.
  • The update supports Pixel 6a through the Pixel 10 lineup, plus the Pixel Fold and Pixel Tablet, and is available via the Android Beta Program and Android Emulator.

Google’s not wasting any time after wrapping up Android 17 QPR1. The company has officially kicked off testing of Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1, offering Pixel users and developers a first look at the next quarterly update expected to arrive as the December 2026 Feature Drop.

This release doesn’t come with any new user-facing features, but it does fix some annoying bugs that have plagued Pixel owners. In its official release notes, Google says it has fixed an issue where Bluetooth devices could fail to reconnect after losing pairing, with no indication there was a problem. There’s also a fix that stops media controls from flashing briefly on the lock screen when waking a phone, even if notifications for that app had been turned off.

The most important fix is maybe for Gemini. Pixel devices may suddenly reboot when launching Google’s AI assistant due to a system crash. Google also addressed an issue with touch input where apps would stop receiving touch events after users performed a drag-and-drop gesture with multiple fingers.

The Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 is now available with build number CP41.260701.005 and supports most of the supported Pixels, including the Pixel 6a, Pixel 7 series, Pixel Fold, Pixel Tablet, Pixel 8 series, Pixel 9 family, and the latest Pixel 10 devices. It’s also available through the Android Emulator, and users enrolled in the program can install it over the air via the Android Beta Program.

But the rollout brings a major change, too. If you still have a Pixel 6 or 6 Pro, your beta journey ends right here. These devices have reached their guaranteed update limits, so the previous QPR1 branch is their final beta track.

Android Central's Take

I don’t mind if Google takes a break from dumping flashy new features on us if it means they’ll actually iron out the bugs that get in the way of using my phone. Not getting access to yet another AI gimmick is 100x more annoying than Gemini demanding a reboot or Bluetooth suddenly refusing to reconnect. That said, I’d still like to see Google spend less time playing catch-up with stability issues in the first place. Pixel owners signed up for a premium Android experience, not a free QA gig. If QPR2 delivers a smoother, more reliable phone by December, that’s a win. But it would be even better if these fixes didn’t have to wait for a quarterly update.

item.title: Leaked Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 specs suggest an upgrade in the graphics department
item.description: A rumor spins an interesting theory for Qualcomm's next budget Snapdragon 3 Gen 6 and its graphical performance.
item.author: nickodiaz@sbcglobal.net (Nickolas Diaz)
item.dc:creator: Nickolas Diaz
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5 SoC helps empower more affordable phones with performance capabilities that make the difference in everyday life.
media:content.media:text: The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5 SoC helps empower more affordable phones with performance capabilities that make the difference in everyday life.
media:content.media:title: The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5 SoC helps empower more affordable phones with performance capabilities that make the difference in everyday life.

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Rumors about Qualcomm's next budget chip, the Snapdragon 4 gen 6, claim another graphical upgrade is in the works.
  • A report claims the chip could feature an Adreno 6-series GPU, as well as Bluetooth 6.0 and Wi-Fi 6 connectivity.
  • The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5, which launched in May, was the first 4-series chip that could muster 90fps in games with an Adreno 4-series GPU.
Android Central's Take

I'm interested in this graphics rumor for the SD 4 Gen 6 the most. I've had budget phones before, and they've done okay—for a time. Games continue to update. Their graphics continue to evolve. Eventually, that budget phone feels... outmatched. Qualcomm's trying to help by offering a better GPU, and 90fps is a good thing to have. I just can't help but wonder how much further it's willing to push its budget chip's GPU since it's, well, budget.

Qualcomm is supposedly working on another generation of chips for budget phones, and its specs might've appeared online.

Budget phones are typically dropped down a notch when it comes to overall performance. However, alleged details about Qualcomm's next Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 from NotebookCheck suggest the company is trying to close the gap. The post claims the SD 4 Gen 6 could include a much-needed Adreno GPU upgrade. The chip might jump to the Adreno 6 series.

It's suspected that this would deliver yet another significant increase in graphical output for budget phones. As it stands, the publication didn't have concrete numbers. Other supposed upgrades involve its security, which is "getting a refresh." As far as connectivity is concerned, rumors state the SD 4 Gen 6 could feature Bluetooth 6.0 and an upgraded Wi-Fi 6 over Wi-Fi 5 in the Gen 5 SoC.

For what might remain the same, the publication adds that the Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 might keep the same CPU architecture as its predecessor. If that's the case, then we might be in for another 2+6 Kyro core arrangement (Kryo Gold, Kryo Silver). Another similarity could be its process, which NotebookCheck says might remain at 4nm.

Qualcomm released a pair of chips in May, both of which focused on the affordable phone market. Graphics were a focus for Qualcomm with the SD 4 Gen 5. The company states this chip features a "77% increase in Adreno GPU performance for 90fps in games." This was a big moment for Qualcomm. Previously, no 4-series chip achieved such a high FPS (frames per second). Mind you, this is with the Adreno 4-series GPU. With rumors suggesting an upgrade in this department, hopefully Qualcomm can push its graphics even more.

Budget phones often struggle with mobile games with more intense graphics, but knocking them down to medium or low can help. However, if Qualcomm is truly looking to boost its GPU again, maybe users can feel more comfortable with medium graphics across the board without noticeable FPS drops.

The rumors about the Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 didn't have a release date in mind. Considering we got Qualcomm's last wave in May, there's a chance the company could stick to that next year.

item.title: AT&T could raise home internet prices for the people who can least afford it
item.description: AT&T is reportedly hiking legacy Fiber and Internet Air bills by $5 again, leaving customers paying more.
item.author: techkritiko@gmail.com (Jay Bonggolto)
item.dc:creator: Jay Bonggolto
item.dc:description: <p>Jay Bonggolto always keeps a nose for news. He has been writing about consumer tech and apps for as long as he can remember, and he has used a variety of Android phones since falling in love with Jelly Bean. When he's not writing, he likes to spend time outside, stealing scenes with his phone camera. Send him a direct message via X or LinkedIn.</p>

media:content.media:description: AT&T logo
media:content.media:text: AT&T logo
media:content.media:title: AT&T logo

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • AT&T is reportedly raising home internet prices again, with legacy Fiber and Internet Air customers facing a $5 monthly increase starting August 16.
  • Promotional discounts will stay, but affected customers will still pay more because the base monthly rate is increasing.
  • AT&T says the extra revenue funds network and service improvements, though customers have heard that explanation before.

AT&T just can’t seem to stop raising prices, and now its home internet customers may be next. The carrier already raised prices on a handful of wireless plans earlier this year and is reportedly planning a second monthly price hike for some internet customers beginning August 16.

PhoneArena has learned from internal documents that the bill of customers on legacy AT&T Fiber and Internet Air plans will be increased by $5 per month starting in mid-August.

Notices are expected to be included on future billing statements before the changes are implemented. Existing promotional discounts should remain in place, but the higher base price means many subscribers will still end up paying more overall.

It’s a move that’s becoming more and more familiar for AT&T customers. Over the last few months, the carrier has bumped up prices on several legacy wireless plans, tacked on more to the rates of retired Unlimited Your Way offerings, and even raised its Administrative and Regulatory Cost Recovery Fee by $1 per line.

AT&T’s explanation, too, sounds familiar. Higher prices help support continued investments in the company’s network, service quality, and customer experience, internal documents say. It's the same explanation it's used for past increases in wireless prices. The question is whether customers will see enough added value to warrant another bill increase.

Android Central's Take

If I’m paying more every few months, I’d like to see something tangible in return for that, rather than the usual promise of “network investments.” Faster speeds, wider coverage, or some meaningful new perks would make the extra $5 easier to swallow. Instead, customers are left wondering again whether they’re being rewarded for loyalty or just taxed.

AT&T has been touting newer offerings, such as its customizable Build-A-Plan service that now bundles wireless and home internet under a single subscription. The carrier isn’t forcing a move, but increasing the price of older plans could naturally push some subscribers to look at newer options.

If you have AT&T internet, look for a price notice on your next bill. The hike won’t be equal for everyone, but if you have an older Fiber or Internet Air plan, your monthly bill could soon jump by another $5. At this point, you may want to compare your current plan to newer offerings, or even competing providers, to see if sticking around still makes financial sense.

item.title: Galaxy S26 Ultra suffers an unusual 'red screen' issue: Samsung says it can fix it
item.description: Galaxy S26 Ultra users started reporting a "redness" on their displays, and Samsung's investigation might've led to a solution.
item.author: nickodiaz@sbcglobal.net (Nickolas Diaz)
item.dc:creator: Nickolas Diaz
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: Testing out the Privacy Display on the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra
media:content.media:text: Testing out the Privacy Display on the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra
media:content.media:title: Testing out the Privacy Display on the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • A South Korean publication highlights a series of reports from Galaxy S26 Ultra users, who claim a "red screen" or "red stain" has affected their displays.
  • The post cites a Samsung official who places the blame on the phone's Privacy Display, stating that a software update is in the works to solve this problem.
  • After the S26 Ultra debuted, tests discovered that the phone's display is darker than the Galaxy S25 Ultra, and the Privacy Display is the cause.

Consumers with a Galaxy S26 Ultra are starting to report a strange display issue with the phone. Samsung supposedly chimed in, and what it said could be a relief to many.

Before the weekend, News1 (Korean) highlighted an issue that Galaxy S26 Ultra users have continued to report (via GSMArena). According to the publication, many consumers have experienced a "red screen" or "red stain" issue with the Ultra's display, leading many to worry. This red screen issue is most noticeable when the phone produces brighter colors, such as white. Many users have experienced a wide area of redness on their display, whether vertically (in a wide strip) or in specific places.

Samsung reportedly investigated these reports and determined that the Galaxy S26 Ultra's "new technology" is the cause. The Korean publication cites a Samsung official who places the blame on how the S26 Ultra's Privacy Display works. The official said that this "red stain" would occur when the phone is "exposed to strong lighting at maximum brightness."

While the problem itself isn't great to have, the fact that Samsung feels the cause is its Privacy Display is. Samsung's Privacy Display is purely software-based. It's for this reason that the post highlights Samsung's message to users, stating a software update is on the way to resolve this problem. The publication mentions that many of these reports were from users who've had their S26 Ultra for "three to four months."

Privacy Display took something, too

If Samsung is pointing fingers at its Privacy Display tech, then we can't help but drum up a known fact: the S26 Ultra's screen has suffered because of it. We're not using this term—"suffered"—in a dramatic way. What we mean is, the S26 Ultra's screen is not as bright as the S25 Ultra's. Our friends at Tom's Guide did a deep dive and found that the newer model's screen is much darker. At wider angles, the S26 Ultra's drop in peak brightness is quite noticeable.

Even Android Central's Nicholas Sutrich discovered the same facts about the S26 Ultra. The phone also offers questionable comfort for people sensitive to PWM dimming. The S26 Ultra still uses a 480Hz rate, while Sutrich says its competitors have moved on to 5,000Hz or higher. Moreover, Samsung hasn't switched to 10-bit displays. The S26 Ultra still clings to 8-bit.

Android Central's Take

The issue the S26 Ultra has is likely more of a concern than people realize. I'm sure some people have taken out their phones to see if their devices are suffering, too. The timeline is just so strange and pinpoint, especially if you got the device right when it launched. My hope is that Samsung's software update not only solves this current issue, but helps ensure it doesn't happen in the future. There's nothing like getting a fix for something, only to have it break again a few months later.

item.title: Verizon is clearing Google Pixel 10 stock by giving away the phones for free — here's how to claim yours
item.description: Add a line with any of Verizon's mainline Unlimited plans and you'll get enough promo credits to make the Google Pixel 10 Pro completely free.
item.author: patrick.farmer@futurenet.com (Patrick Farmer)
item.dc:creator: Patrick Farmer
item.dc:description: <p>Patrick (he/him) is the eCommerce Editor of Android Central. After working in independent bookstores for the better part of a decade and using freelance writing as a side hustle, Patrick switched to writing full time in 2020. Patrick’s writing has appeared in the Chicago Tribune, The Hill, BestReviews, OddityMall, and many other publications across the web. He joined Android Central as the Deals and Commerce Writer in 2022 and now runs the site’s eCommerce efforts and coverage of major sale events. Although he’s written about everything from exercise equipment to turntables, Patrick’s work nowadays is primarily concerned with saving people money on tech. He’s particularly passionate about eliminating buyer’s remorse and taking subjects that can be confusing for shoppers — such as switching wireless carriers or buying a new smart TV — and breaking them down into simple, easy-to-understand terms. Whether you’re a veteran tech-head or a novice, Patrick offers honest, no-nonsense shopping advice with years of hands-on experience to back it up. When he isn’t hunting down the best discounts or digging through the fine print of a carrier deal, Patrick enjoys trekking around the Rocky Mountains, camping out at a brewery, or spending time with his beloved polydactyl cat. &nbsp;</p>

media:content.media:description: The back of the jade Google Pixel 10 Pro
media:content.media:text: The back of the jade Google Pixel 10 Pro
media:content.media:title: The back of the jade Google Pixel 10 Pro

item.content:encoded:
The Google Pixel 11 series is set to be announced on August 12th, but with back to school season upon us, you may not want to wait any longer to buy that smartphone you've been eyeing.

Last year's Google Pixel 10 Pro continues to reign as one of the best Android phones on the market today, and now you can get one for FREE by adding a new line with an eligible data plan at Verizon.

The carrier's Unlimited Welcome, Unlimited Plus, and Unlimited Ultimate plans will all earn you the full $999.99 in savings over 36 months, making this one of the best Verizon deals I've seen all year. No trade-in required!

Google Pixel 10 Pro 128GB: $999.99 FREE with eligible unlimited plan at Verizon

Need a new phone for the upcoming school year? The Google Pixel 10 Pro boasts a bounty of powerful hardware and sophisticated design choices, and now you can get the AI-boosted flagship for free by adding a line with Verizon's Unlimited Welcome, Unlimited Plus, or Unlimited Ultimate plans. View Deal

Although many new phone releases feel somewhat iterative these days, Google bucked that trend by making the Pixel 10 Pro a significant upgrade from its predecessor. Straight out of the box, the phone boasts a lovely and compact 6.3-inch pOLED display — the same size as the base model Pixel 10 — while under the hood you get a hefty 16GB of RAM, outstanding performance from the Tensor G5 processor, and seven years of guaranteed OS and security upgrades.

The Pixel 10 Pro also comes packed with the latest exclusive AI-boosted software features from Gemini, including Voice Translate for Calls (a godsend when traveling), Magic Cue, Edit with Ask Photos, and more. Moreover, you get all of the features we've come to expect from Pixel phones, like excellent haptics and versatile camera tech, plus you get to enjoy some new additions like Pixelsnap Qi2 support.

All things said and done, if you're a Verizon user (or willing to switch), this is an excellent Google Pixel 10 Pro deal that comes with surprisingly little fine print attached. Sure, buying an unlocked device may be more straightforward, but if you need a new wireless plan any way, why not give Verizon a shot and get an incredible Android phone out of the deal??

item.content:encoded:
Summer sale season is here, which means many of our favorite retailers are dropping sweet deals on Android tech, with options that range from discounted phones and smartwatches to tablets and TVs. Not one to be outdone by the competition, Best Buy has just launched its annual 'Black Friday in July' sale, and I've done the work of gathering the TOP 10 best deals for Android Central readers so you don't have to go searching.

The deals on display at Best Buy today include things like $300 off the Google Pixel 10 Pro, one of our favorite Android phones around, plus this offer that drops a whopping 53% off this 77-inch OLED TV by LG. 

There are plenty more discounts where those came from, so keep reading for all of my top picks from the sale. And don't forget: Best Buy's 'Black Friday in July' sale ends on Sunday, July 26th, so don't wait too long if a particular price drop catches your eye. 

  • See the full list of deals at Best Buy today

The TOP 10 deals from Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale

1. Google Pixel 10 Pro 128GB: $999 $699 at Best Buy

The Google Pixel 10 Pro is still one of our favorite Android phones on the market today, coming complete with a lightning-fast Tensor G5 processor, a ton of handy AI features, and some of the most versatile cameras we've ever used. Buy the 128GB flagship unlocked from Best Buy today and you'll score a straight $300 off your purchase. View Deal

2. LG 77" Class B5 Series OLED TV: $2,999.99 $1,399.99 at Best Buy

LG's B5 Series is widely considered to be one of the best entry-level TVs in the OLED space, and now you can save over 50% off the 77-inch model when you make your purchase during Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale. View Deal

3. Toshiba 65" Class C350 Series 4K UHD Fire TV: $529.99 $249.99 at Best Buy

If you don't want to shell out a ton of cash for a new OLED TV, Toshiba's Class C350 Series presents an excellent alternative. Currently just $249.99 at Best Buy, this 65-inch set boasts 4K UHD picture with AI upscaling and the Fire TV entertainment platform. View Deal

4. Google Pixel Watch 4 (45mm) LTE: $499.99 $399.99 at Best Buy

If you don't want to wait for the Pixel Watch 5 unveiling on August 12th, last year's Google Pixel Watch 4 is still an exceptional Wear OS watch, especially when you slap on a straight 20% discount at Best Buy. View Deal

5. Motorola Razr Ultra (2025) 512GB: $1,299.99 $699.99 at Best Buy

Although it's technically a last-gen device, the 2025 Motorola Razr Ultra is still one of the best flip phones you can buy today, with a titanium-reinforced hinge, powerful Snapdragon chip, and 16GB of RAM with 512GB of onboard storage. Grab the clamshell device from Best Buy today and you'll score a whopping $600 off your purchase, no strings attached. View Deal

6. Amazon Kindle Paperwhite Signature Edition: $199.99 $144.99 at Best Buy

The Kindle Paperwhite is one of our all-time favorite e-readers, and now you can get the Signature Edition with a straight $55 discount during Best Buy's big Black Friday in July sale. This portable tablet features weeks of battery life with an adjustable display, wireless charging support, and 32GB of storage for all of your favorite titles. View Deal

7. Insignia 50" Class F50 Series 4K Fire TV: $299.99 $169.99 at Best Buy

Insignia is famous for producing high-quality smart TVs that won't break the bank, and now you can get this 50-inch Class F50 Series set for only $170 during Best Buy's big sale. View Deal

8. Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra 256GB: $1,299.99 $849.99 at Best Buy

The Galaxy S25 Ultra may not be the newest kid on the block any more, but it's still an excellent Samsung phone with powerful Snapdragon performance, versatile camera tech, and years of guaranteed software support. The phone was pretty pricey at launch, but now you can save $450 by buying the S25 Ultra unlocked at Best Buy. View Deal

9. HP 14" Chromebook 64GB eMMC / 4GB RAM: $399 $169 at Best Buy

The HP Chromebook 14 is a solid laptop with a sturdy build, all-day battery, and an efficient Intel Celeron processor that should handle most regular tasks with ease. Grab the productivity machine during Best Buy's sale and you'll score over 50% off your purchase!View Deal

10. Garmin Instinct E (45mm) GPS: $249.99 $199.99 at Best Buy

The Garmin Instinct E is a rugged wearable with military-grade durability, up to 16 days of battery life, and all of the 24/7 health and fitness tracking capabilities that have made the Garmin brand name so famous. Pick up the watch during Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale and you'll enjoy a straight $50 off your purchase. View Deal

item.title: Android Auto is finally getting the Google Maps feature drivers have wanted
item.description: Android Auto is finally getting a live, color-changing speedometer in Google Maps after years of waiting, but you'll have to sit tight while this long-overdue update slowly rolls out.
item.author: techkritiko@gmail.com (Jay Bonggolto)
item.dc:creator: Jay Bonggolto
item.dc:description: <p>Jay Bonggolto always keeps a nose for news. He has been writing about consumer tech and apps for as long as he can remember, and he has used a variety of Android phones since falling in love with Jelly Bean. When he's not writing, he likes to spend time outside, stealing scenes with his phone camera. Send him a direct message via X or LinkedIn.</p>

media:content.media:description: Android Auto running split-screen with Waze in Mercedes.
media:content.media:text: Android Auto running split-screen with Waze in Mercedes.
media:content.media:title: Android Auto running split-screen with Waze in Mercedes.

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Google Maps is finally bringing a live speedometer to Android Auto, closing a long-standing feature gap with Android phones and Apple CarPlay.
  • The rollout is currently very limited, with reports pointing to a server-side release for select Google Maps beta users on version 26.29.02.946673643.
  • The speedometer appears next to the speed limit indicator and changes color when you exceed the posted limit, making it easier to monitor your speed at a glance.

Google is finally checking off one of Android Auto users’ oldest wish list items. Google Maps has had a live speedometer feature on Android phones for a long time and even brought it to Apple CarPlay in 2024, but now it’s starting to show your speed on Android Auto as well.

It seems like the rollout is very limited for now. User reports from Reddit suggest that the feature is rolling out to a small number of beta users running Google Maps version 26.29.02.946673643, including at least one driver in Canada (via 9to5Google). As is often the case with recent Google Maps features, the update seems to be a server-side rollout, so simply updating the app doesn’t mean you’ll get it immediately.

The speedometer sits alongside the speed limit indicator when that information is available. Community reports say the speed read-out changes color as well if you’re driving over the posted limit, making it easier to spot at a glance without taking your eyes off the road for long.

As with everything, Google considers the feature a reference and not a substitute for the real speedometer in your vehicle.

The delay has been particularly puzzling, given that Android Auto has lagged behind both Google Maps on Android phones and Apple’s CarPlay, despite them sharing a lot of the same navigation experience.

With this update finally arriving, Android Auto is edging closer to feature parity as Google continues rolling out other navigation upgrades, including its new immersive Maps interface with 3D buildings, improved lane guidance, and easier-to-follow highway interchanges.

Android Central's Take

This is one of those updates that, for me, deserves a “finally” more than a “yay.” A live speedometer isn’t some new AI-powered thing; it’s a simple navigation feature that’s been on phones and even CarPlay for ages. I’m still pleased to see it coming to Android Auto, because it’ll give drivers one less reason to look down at the dashboard, especially when driving in unfamiliar areas with lots of speed limit changes. I just wish Google would be just as quick about practical quality-of-life features as they are with fancy add-ons.

item.title: Don't need a 300W charging station? These 4 features will change your mind
item.description: Cuktech's 30 Ultra is a 300W charging station designed for your desk. It does 140W out of two USB-C ports, and has the ability to charge all your devices — at the same time.
item.author: harish.jonnalagadda@futurenet.com (Harish Jonnalagadda)
item.dc:creator: Harish Jonnalagadda
item.dc:description: <p>Harish Jonnalagadda is Android Central's Senior Editor overseeing the mobile category. He started his tech journey at a very young age by tweaking Windows XP installations, and that hobby ignited an enthusiasm that led him into the world of PC modding. After picking up a Bachelor's degree in computer science, he decided to pursue his passion by covering PC hardware at VR-Zone, where he reviewed motherboards, video cards, and DRAM modules.</p><p><br>While he used iPhones initially, switching to the Nexus 4 served as the catalyst to explore Android's burgeoning ecosystem, and he pivoted to writing about mobile tech, joining Android Central's newsroom back in 2014. As a Senior Editor, he oversees the site's coverage of Chinese phone brands, enthusiast audio products, networking hardware, and storage servers, leveraging his engineering background and extensive experience testing mobile hardware to evaluate the latest phones and accessories.<br></p><p>In over a decade of covering Android, he has used pretty much every single major phone released globally, giving him an unrivaled view into Google's mobile platform. His specialty is Chinese brands; he's charted the growth of all the major Chinese manufacturers from their inception, and he uses that knowledge to share unique insights. When not testing the latest gadgets, he can be found reading sci-fi novels on his e-reader, and occasionally yelling at the TV in frustration as Arsenal squander yet another title run.</p>

media:content.media:description: Cuktech 30 Ultra charging station review on Android Central
media:content.media:text: Cuktech 30 Ultra charging station review on Android Central
media:content.media:title: Cuktech 30 Ultra charging station review on Android Central

item.content:encoded:
Charging devices can get out of hand, particularly if you have a few phones, notebooks, tablets, and accessories. You can just use the charger that comes in the box with the phone (which in itself is vanishingly rare these days), but a much better choice is to get a dedicated charging station. Going this route will allow you to charge all of your devices at once, and you get much more power than with a single charger — I use UGREEN's Nexode 500W charging station, and its six ports allow me to charge all of my tech with ease.

Cuktech is a Chinese charging brand that's slowly gaining momentum; I use its 15 Ultra power bank and 140W charger daily, and its products are just as reliable as UGREEN and Anker. The brand is now making charging stations, and I've been testing the 300W 30 Ultra for the better part of a month now. The 30 Ultra retails for $159 on Amazon, and while that is costly, you are getting a charger that can easily charge up to five devices at once. To put this into context, the Nexode 500W that I use in my office costs $199.

If you're not quite sure about what you get with a charging station, these are four things I liked the most about the Cuktech 30 Ultra after a month of testing.

The Cuktech 30 Ultra has all the ports you need

Obviously, the biggest reason for buying a GaN charging station is to be able to charge several devices at once. The 30 Ultra has three USB-C ports, one USB-A port, and a DC connector on the side that lets you connect to notebooks.

The charger also gets a wedge-style stand that angles it on a desk, and this makes the accessing the ports a little better.

When it comes to USB-C, you get 140W over the USB PD 3.1 protocol with the USB-C1 and C2 ports, and the best part is that both ports can deliver 140W at once. This means you can effortlessly charge two MacBooks simultaneously with the 30 Ultra, and that's a big advantage.

The USB-C3 port goes up to 33W, and the USB-A port hits 18W. Cuktech bundles a 240W USB-C to USB-C cable in the box, and you can use it to charge just about any gadget.

It has (almost) all mainstream charging protocols

Most charging stations just get USB PD and leave it at that, so it's great to see Cuktech add a lot of additional charging protocols. That includes Xiaomi's 120W charging standard, and I tested it with the Xiaomi 17 Ultra and Xiaomi 17T Pro to confirm. You also get 100W USB PD with PPS, Qualcomm's QC3.0 and QC2.0, and Huawei's fast charging standard.

The only standard missing is OPPO's VOOC, and I wasn't able to charge my Find X9 Ultra to its potential. That said, the phone goes up to 50W over USB PD, and this proved to be handy with the charger.

You get a dedicated DC port

It's the versatility with the DC connector that gives the 30 Ultra an edge over other charging stations. This port goes up to 300W, so you can easily charge power-hungry gaming notebooks without too much of a hassle. You get a smattering of connectors that you can use with the DC port, but note that these will only work when you connect to a notebook.

There's a built-in LCD panel

While most chargers come with a built-in panel these days — I like the ChargeView 140W because of this — Cuktech took it to the next level with the 30 Ultra charging station. It gets a bigger 1.83-inch panel, and it shows real-time charging details, a power curve, and even how much current is going out of a cable.

It's a valuable addition if you've connected several gadgets to the charging station and want to see just how fast individual devices are charging.

Overall, I really liked using the 30 Ultra; Cuktech nailed the design and the port selection, and having two USB-C ports with 140W power is a welcome move. The addition of a DC port is great as well, and the 1.83-inch panel is a nifty inclusion that lets you see in real-time how fast connected devices are charging. If you need a way to declutter your desk with a single charger, the 30 Ultra is a good choice in 2026.

Cuktech
30 Ultra 300W Charging Station

The 30 Ultra makes it easier to charge several devices at once, and the selection of ports and charging standards means you can plug in all of your devices at once.

item.title: Google Pixel 11 Pro Fold vs. Motorola Razr Fold: What's the best foldable in the US?
item.description: Motorola's Razr Fold is one of the best foldables available today. So what does Google need to do with the Pixel 11 Pro Fold to be able to measure up? Let's take a look.
item.author: harish.jonnalagadda@futurenet.com (Harish Jonnalagadda)
item.dc:creator: Harish Jonnalagadda
item.dc:description: <p>Harish Jonnalagadda is Android Central's Senior Editor overseeing the mobile category. He started his tech journey at a very young age by tweaking Windows XP installations, and that hobby ignited an enthusiasm that led him into the world of PC modding. After picking up a Bachelor's degree in computer science, he decided to pursue his passion by covering PC hardware at VR-Zone, where he reviewed motherboards, video cards, and DRAM modules.</p><p><br>While he used iPhones initially, switching to the Nexus 4 served as the catalyst to explore Android's burgeoning ecosystem, and he pivoted to writing about mobile tech, joining Android Central's newsroom back in 2014. As a Senior Editor, he oversees the site's coverage of Chinese phone brands, enthusiast audio products, networking hardware, and storage servers, leveraging his engineering background and extensive experience testing mobile hardware to evaluate the latest phones and accessories.<br></p><p>In over a decade of covering Android, he has used pretty much every single major phone released globally, giving him an unrivaled view into Google's mobile platform. His specialty is Chinese brands; he's charted the growth of all the major Chinese manufacturers from their inception, and he uses that knowledge to share unique insights. When not testing the latest gadgets, he can be found reading sci-fi novels on his e-reader, and occasionally yelling at the TV in frustration as Arsenal squander yet another title run.</p>

media:content.media:description: Hands-on with the Motorola Razr Fold alongside the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 7 at MWC 2026.
media:content.media:text: Hands-on with the Motorola Razr Fold alongside the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 7 at MWC 2026.
media:content.media:title: Hands-on with the Motorola Razr Fold alongside the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 7 at MWC 2026.

item.content:encoded:
Google
Pixel 11 Pro Fold

All-round upgrades

Google is slated to bring a ton of exciting upgrades to the Pixel 11 Pro Fold. It is touted to be thinner and lighter, and the switch to a brand-new 2nm platform should solve long-standing performance issues. With Google also set to roll out new imaging modules, the foldable should take great photos. It will need to do all these things to hold its own against the Razr Fold.

Motorola
Razr Fold

The one to beat

Motorola did all the right things with the Razr Fold. It has an ultra-thin design, great cameras, one of the biggest batteries of any phone sold in the U.S., and fast charging tech. The software is a joy to use, and all things considered, this is one of the best foldables available today.

Google Pixel 11 Pro Fold vs. Motorola Razr Fold: Pricing and availability

Google is all set to launch the Pixel 11 Pro Fold on August 12, and that is when we'll get official confirmation of what the foldable will cost. That said, a massive Pixel 11 leak gave us a good indication of the pricing, and the Pixel 11 Pro Fold is set to cost $1,899 for the 256GB model.

Coincidentally, the Razr Fold also retails for $1,899 in the U.S., but you're getting 512GB of storage as standard. While that's a lot of cash towards a phone, you get decent trade-in deals and carrier subsidies, so you don't have to shell out the entire amount in one go unless you're buying unlocked.

Google Pixel 11 Pro Fold vs. Motorola Razr Fold: Design

I really like Google's design aesthetic, but I will admit that Motorola did a great job with the Razr Fold. The Pixel 10 Pro Fold was unwieldy to use because of its weight and size, and thankfully, the Pixel 11 Pro Fold is set to address that; Google is said to be shaving the weight and thickness of the foldable. That's good news, because it would make the Pixel 11 Pro Fold in line with its immediate rivals.

The Razr Fold has a distinctive design thanks to the camera island, and I like Motorola's color and materials choices. The foldable looks and feels like a high-end device, and while it is heavy at 243, the weight is distributed evenly, so it isn't uncomfortable to hold. Another plus point is that the Razr Fold is just as thin as the Galaxy Z Fold 7 and the Find N6, and this makes holding and using it that much more enjoyable.

Where the Pixel 11 Pro Fold should have an advantage is ingress protection; it should retain IP68 dust and water resistance that we saw on the Pixel 10 Pro Fold. Motorola also does a decent enough job, with the Razr Fold getting IP48 and IP49 resistance.

Google Pixel 11 Pro Fold vs. Motorola Razr Fold: Specs

Google is finally set to address long-standing issues with its Pixels with the introduction of the Pixel 11. This is by way of the Tensor G6 platform, which is built on a new 2nm node. The node shift will bring inherent efficiency gains, but what I'm excited about is the switch to new cores, which will allow the Pixel 11 Pro Fold to be that much faster — and better at gaming. This has been a sore point on Google's devices for a long time now, and I can't wait to get my hands on the Pixel 11 Pro Fold to see how it holds up against the likes of the Find N6 and Vivo X Fold 5.

If anything, Motorola also went a conservative route with the Razr Fold. Instead of Qualcomm's best silicon, the foldable gets the Snapdragon 8 Gen 5, so it doesn't quite have the best possible chipset available. That said, it isn't slow in daily use, and it does a decent job with demanding games as well. But it just rubs me the wrong way to see anything other than flagship silicon on an $1,899 phone.

Category

Google Pixel 11 Pro Fold (based on leaks)

Motorola Razr Fold

Operating System

Android 17, seven years of software updates (likely)

Android 16, up to seven years of software updates

Display

Outer: 6.4-inch OLED, 120Hz, 3500 nits

Inner: 8.0-inch OLED, 120Hz, 3500 nits

Outer: 6.6-inch OLED, 165Hz,

Inner: 8.1-inch OLED, 120Hz

Chipset

Tensor G6, 2nm TSMC

Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 5

RAM

16GB

12GB/16GB

Storage

256GB/512GB/1TB

256GB/512GB/1TB

Rear cameras

Main: 50MP

Tele: 10.8MP f/3.1, OIS, 5x zoom

Wide: 10.5MP f/2.2

Main: 50MP f/1.6, OIS

Tele: 50MP f/2.4, OIS, 3x optical zoom

Wide: 50MP f/2.0

Front camera

Outer: 10MP f/2.2

Inner: 10MP f/2.2

Outer: 20MP f/2.4

Inner: 32MP f/2.4

Battery

4,800mAh

6,000mAh

Charging

45W wired, PPS

15W Qi 2 wireless

80W wired, PPS

50W wireless

Ingress protection

IP68 dust and water resistance

IP48 and IP49 dust and water resistance

Now, where the Razr Fold has an unquestionable advantage is with battery life. Thanks to a massive 6,000mAh battery, the foldables lasts over a day regularly. What's also great is that Motorola is using silicon-carbon tech, which we just haven't seen a lot of in the U.S. until now. This allows better density and durability, and the Razr Fold gets much better charging as well: it uses Motorola's 80W tech.

Google is said to be lowering the battery size on the Pixel 11 Pro Fold to 4,800mAh, and I think this is the wrong move. Of course, we will need to wait and see if the leaks pan out, but a foldable needs to have at least a 5,000mAh battery, and I want Google to do better this year.

Similarly, cameras are an area where the Pixel 11 Pro Fold must do better than its predecessor. Take the Razr Fold; Motorola outfitted it with the same cameras as the Signature, and it does an excellent job with photos and videos.

Google Pixel 11 Pro Fold vs. Motorola Razr Fold: Which should you buy?

With the Pixel 11 Pro Fold projected to cost about the same as the Razr Fold, pricing isn't going to be the point of difference. Instead, it will be about the features. I really like what Motorola did with the Razr Fold; it has a great design, standout cameras, and a huge battery.

Google will need to offer those things with the Pixel 11 Pro Fold as well — these are table stakes features at this point. The software is also a big selling point, and Motorola does well here too; its UI is clean, and you get a decent amount of extras. That said, Google has a sizeable edge with the software, and I'm excited to see what the Pixel 11 Pro Fold has to deliver in this area.

Motorola
Razr Fold

The one to beat

The Pixel 11 Pro Fold is launching soon, but if you don't want to wait, the Razr Fold is the obvious choice if you're in the U.S. — this is the foldable I would buy in 2026.

item.title: 3 reasons why this 140W GaN charger took over my desk
item.description: Satechi just made the ideal 4-port GaN charger. It is smaller than all of its rivals, has 140W charging, and there's even a built-in panel that shows real-time charging details.
item.author: harish.jonnalagadda@futurenet.com (Harish Jonnalagadda)
item.dc:creator: Harish Jonnalagadda
item.dc:description: <p>Harish Jonnalagadda is Android Central's Senior Editor overseeing the mobile category. He started his tech journey at a very young age by tweaking Windows XP installations, and that hobby ignited an enthusiasm that led him into the world of PC modding. After picking up a Bachelor's degree in computer science, he decided to pursue his passion by covering PC hardware at VR-Zone, where he reviewed motherboards, video cards, and DRAM modules.</p><p><br>While he used iPhones initially, switching to the Nexus 4 served as the catalyst to explore Android's burgeoning ecosystem, and he pivoted to writing about mobile tech, joining Android Central's newsroom back in 2014. As a Senior Editor, he oversees the site's coverage of Chinese phone brands, enthusiast audio products, networking hardware, and storage servers, leveraging his engineering background and extensive experience testing mobile hardware to evaluate the latest phones and accessories.<br></p><p>In over a decade of covering Android, he has used pretty much every single major phone released globally, giving him an unrivaled view into Google's mobile platform. His specialty is Chinese brands; he's charted the growth of all the major Chinese manufacturers from their inception, and he uses that knowledge to share unique insights. When not testing the latest gadgets, he can be found reading sci-fi novels on his e-reader, and occasionally yelling at the TV in frustration as Arsenal squander yet another title run.</p>

media:content.media:description: Satechi ChargeView 140W GaN charger review on Android Central
media:content.media:text: Satechi ChargeView 140W GaN charger review on Android Central
media:content.media:title: Satechi ChargeView 140W GaN charger review on Android Central

item.content:encoded:
I use several chargers throughout the house, and I still rely on the 500W Nexode as my go-to charging station in the office. As good as it is, the charging station is bulky, so I don't use it on my desk. I instead prefer a smaller charger that still gives me the flexibility to charge up to four devices at once, and in recent months, I used Baseus' excellent Nomos 2, which also has a Qi 2.2 mat.

That was before Satechi rolled out its latest GaN charger. The ChargeView 140W is a 4-port charger that's roughly the same size as a regular 140W wall charger, but you get four charging ports in total, and Satechi even managed to fit a tiny panel onto it that shows real-time charging details. The best part is that it costs just $99 on Amazon, which is similar to other 140W chargers I used in recent months. Here are three reasons why the ChargeView 140W is now my go-to desk charger.

The ChargeView 140W is the smallest 4-port charger I used

For me, the number one consideration for a desk charger is the size, and the ChargeView 140W nails the brief. It is smaller than other 4-port charging stations I used, and if anything, it's even smaller than a travel charger.

I also like that it has four USB-C ports; most 4-port chargers include a USB-A port, and while I can understand the reasoning behind that, I don't need the port on a desk charger, so just having a multitude of USB-C ports is highly convenient — at least in my use.

Satechi also includes a stand that lets you orient the ChargeView 140W horizontally or vertically. I used it horizontally, and the stand makes accessing the USB-C ports just a tad more convenient. The charger itself is made out of plastic, and I didn't see any issues with build quality whatsoever.

It has USB 3.2 AVS and goes up to 140W

The ChargeView 140W does a solid job charging four devices at once, and the C1/C2 ports have the ability to charge devices up to 140W. The USB-C1 port uses the latest USB PD 3.2 AVS standard to adjust voltage in minute increments, and it makes a difference while charging the iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone Air.

The USB-C2 port uses the standard USB PD 3.1 profile, and it also goes up to 140W. The C3 and C4 ports go up to a maximum of 40W individually, and these are better-suited for charging handheld consoles and accessories. Like all other GaN chargers, the ChargeView 140W does a good job intelligently managing the power budget based on the number of ports in use. Here's a look at how the power delivering is affected based on number of devices connected:

I used the ChargeView 140W with my Find X9 Ultra, iPad Pro M4, iPhone 17 Pro, and a dozen accessories over the course of the last month. It did just as reliable a job as other GaN chargers I used, and the smaller size and availability of four USB-C ports makes it a long-term addition to my desk.

You get an LCD panel that shows real-time charging info

It isn't that long ago that brands started adding LCD panels to their chargers to show charging details, and I like this feature a lot. The ChargeView 140W has a tiny panel of its own, and it is a great way to see how fast a connected device is charging.

What I like about this is that you can charge the orientation of the panel based on the charger's configuration. So if you're using it vertically, a long press of the button located at the top switches the orientation. It's a small addition, but it's one that makes a noticeable difference.

On the whole, the ChargeView 140W is a great addition to Satechi's charging portfolio, and if you need a reliable desk charger with the ability to charge four devices at once, I'd suggest getting this one.

Satechi
ChargeView 140W Desktop Charger

The ChargeView 140W is a diminutive 140W GaN charger that effortlessly charges all your tech. It uses the latest USB PD standard, doesn't take up much room at all, and you don't have to pay a premium.

item.title: Stop tap tap tapping on your Android keyboard and get the message through faster with this ancient but underrated feature
item.description: Type faster than ever before on your Android phone using this one hack that helped me increase my typing speed.
item.author: namerah.saud-fatmi@futurenet.com (Namerah Saud Fatmi)
item.dc:creator: Namerah Saud Fatmi
item.dc:description: <p>Namerah Saud Fatmi has been interested in technology ever since the days of the OG Razr Flip and back when the PlayStation Portable was the sickest console to have ever existed. Having fiddled with gadgets, PC parts, and techy tidbits since her early tweens, Namerah started sharing her expertise and opinions with the world in late 2018. Fast forward to today and she has grown into an even more curious — and seasoned — tech enthusiast with a penchant for experimenting with accessories, phones, and mobile devices. Her coverage as a Senior Editor at Android Central focuses on the world of mobile phone accessories, occasionally branching out into sects like gaming, software, hardware, and more. Before becoming a full-time writer for Android Central, Namerah was a freelance writer with bylines at other respectable publications such as XDA Developers, Neowin, and Good e-Reader. She also helps out Android Central's sister brands Windows Central and iMore on and off. Namerah is based out of Malaysia, where she lives with her four-legged life partner, a black cat named Bruce Wayne. Her hobbies include casual gaming, attempting to pet anything classified as an animal, chugging coffee, traveling profusely, and going on culinary adventures whenever possible.</p>

media:content.media:description: Gboard translate keyboard on Android phone.
media:content.media:text: Gboard translate keyboard on Android phone.
media:content.media:title: Gboard translate keyboard on Android phone.

item.content:encoded:
Now that smartphones have become our de facto communication and note-taking devices, the need to type faster has become a priority for many. Whether it's a requirement for your job or it's a skill that'll help you in university or at an internship, I have valuable advice for you.

Many people still default to tapping each letter on their phones, but there's a better way. I learned how to type insanely quickly on my Android phone with a single hack, and while it's not a new feature by any means, I'm going to teach you, too. Once you've mastered this skill, your average words per minute are going to jump up.

How to type really fast on your Android phone

This demonstration was conducted on a Google Pixel 8 running Android 16 and using Gboard as the default keyboard. After explaining how to use this hack, I'll also explain how to implement it in Samsung Keyboard.

As long as you're using Gboard on an Android phone running at least Android 8, you're good to go. This "trick" is called swipe typing, sliding to type, or Glide Typing (on Gboard). The idea is that you don't lift your finger for each letter of the alphabet, only for each word, saving you a lot of time. Here's how to slide your finger across the keyboard to type faster.

1. Tap a text box where you want to type on your phone.

2. Instead of individually tapping the alphabet, slide your finger without lifting it. Pause on the letters for a microsecond, and lift your finger when you're done typing a word. A space is automatically added once you lift your finger.

If you're a Samsung Keyboard user, you'll need to head to the keyboard settings and enable the Swype mode before sliding your finger to type.

Swipe typing on Gboard is intelligent and intuitive

Gboard gets smarter over time, as does your swipe-typing accuracy. As you swipe over the alphabet, you'll get word suggestions in Gboard. Over time, you can teach Gboard what your intended word was by tapping the right suggestion.

Swipe typing also works with words added to your dictionary, such as names, slang, abbreviations, or words in another language. They don't have to be grammatically correct.

What's even cooler is that you can swipe to type in other languages too! I tried it with Urdu in Gboard, and it works just as well as it does in English. Plus, if you use a Wear OS watch with Gboard, you can use this hack on your smartwatch as well.

I attract so much attention when I swipe-text in public, and people are always curious about it. I've even had strangers ask me to teach them how to use the feature on a train!

It is not the fastest or easiest trick right away; you might actually type slower at first. But once you get the hang of it, it's very hard to go back to the "usual" typing method on your smartphone.

item.title: Samsung Galaxy Unpacked 2026 launch live: Z Fold 8 Ultra, Z Fold 8, Z Flip 8, Galaxy Glasses, and all the news
item.description: Samsung is set to launch the new Z Fold 8 Ultra, Z Fold 8, and Z Flip 8, with other devices potentially coming as well. Android Central is following the news through our Live Blog.
item.author: derrek.lee@futurenet.com (Derrek Lee)
item.dc:creator: Derrek Lee
item.dc:description: <p>Derrek has had a long-time interest in mobile technology since the age of 12, which came out of his love for Nokia phones, particularly models like the Nokia N90. Since then, he closely followed the evolution of tech and the transition of Nokia from Symbian to Windows Phone, which eventually led him to Android with phones like the Moto X Pure Edition and LG G5. Since 2013, Derrek has dipped in and out of tech journalism as nothing more than a passion project while receiving his Bachelor of Arts degree in Film &amp; Digital Media at the University of California, Santa Cruz. That is until 2020, when he became the news editor for Android Central, guiding the team's coverage of Google, Android, Wear OS, and related topics. Now, as managing editor, Derrek guides the site's editorial content and direction, helping the team reach and resonate with readers, old and new, who are just as passionate about tech as we are.</p> <p>Throughout his time at Android Central, he has reviewed many products, from phones to smartwatches, smart scales, and even smart rings, establishing himself as an expert with these devices. As a fitness enthusiast, Derrek always looks for ways to incorporate tech into his life by closely examining the intersection of fitness and tech. He is also very passionate about tech being financially accessible, which often drives his decision-making in the types of products he tests out and what he recommends.</p>

media:content.media:description: A Z Fold 7 opening up showcasing lloyd to say that the Galaxy Unpacked live blog is happening
media:content.media:text: A Z Fold 7 opening up showcasing lloyd to say that the Galaxy Unpacked live blog is happening
media:content.media:title: A Z Fold 7 opening up showcasing lloyd to say that the Galaxy Unpacked live blog is happening

item.content:encoded:
Samsung has officially confirmed that it will host its next Galaxy Unpacked event on July 22, 2026, in London at 2 p.m. BST (9 a.m. ET). Managing Editor Derrek Lee will be in the U.K for the event and is ready to report on all things Summer Unpacked!

The company has previously preferred to hold its launch events mostly in the U.S., and occasionally in Korea and even France.

Now, nearly 14 years after the Galaxy S3 event, Samsung is bringing its launch event back to London, England. We're expecting a new range of foldable phones and new Galaxy wearables at the event.

What devices are launching?

Samsung expanded its Galaxy foldable lineup last year with the introduction of the Galaxy Z Flip 7 FE, alongside the standard Galaxy Z Fold 7 and Galaxy Z Flip 7.

This year, the company is expected to switch up the lineup, including successors to last year's phones as well as a brand-new form factor with a passport-like design that's shorter and wider, which will reportedly go head-to-head against the rumoured Apple's foldable iPhone.

Along with launching a new foldable form factor, Samsung is also expected to change its naming scheme in the most confusing way possible. You'd expect the Galaxy Z Fold 7's successor to be called the Galaxy Z Fold 8, but instead, it'll reportedly be named the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra.

The new passport-style foldable will instead take on the standard Galaxy Z Fold 8 name.

We're not sure why Samsung is shuffling things around like this, but we'll certainly be asking around at the event.

Alongside the foldables, Samsung is also expected to launch the Galaxy Watch 9. We are also expecting news around Samsung's AI glasses too!

Interestingly, it looks like we’re finally getting a Galaxy Watch Ultra 2.

Where to watch Samsung Galaxy Unpacked?

There are three ways to watch Samsung's Galaxy Unpacked event live. You can tune in via Samsung's official websites, including Samsung.com and Samsung Newsroom.

Perhaps the easiest way to watch the event is on YouTube. Head to Samsung's official YouTube channel and tap the Notify Me button to be reminded when the event is about to start. Or you can just do it from the video link below.

As always, renders of Samsung's new foldable phones have already leaked ahead of the launch, giving us an idea of what to expect from this new form factor. The leaked images suggest it will be quite different from the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra. Of course, it will be shorter and wider when opened, mimicking a true mini tablet form factor. But there are a few other interesting things to note as well. First, Samsung isn't letting go of its sharp-edged design for this foldable, and we don't yet know whether that wider cover display will dig into your palms too much. Second, you can see on the back that it features only two cameras, compared to the Z Fold 8 Ultra, which will reportedly stick with a triple-camera module.

Alongside the renders, the specs of the standard Galaxy Z Fold 8 have also leaked. The passport-style foldable will reportedly feature a 5.4- to 5.5-inch cover screen, while the inner display measures 7.6 inches. In terms of design, it'll reportedly be just 5.5mm thick when unfolded and 9.7mm when folded. As for internals, you can expect the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 for Galaxy chipset, 12GB of RAM, and 256GB of starting storage. The good news is that Samsung isn't skimping on the battery front, with the Galaxy Z Fold 8 reportedly packing a 4,800mAh cell, which is bigger than last year's Galaxy Z Fold 7. In terms of colors, you can expect Cream, Graphite, and Lavender, along with a reported Pistachio option that'll be a Samsung.com exclusive.

Samsung has already begun teasing what we can expect from its new Galaxy Watches, including “AI-driven insights in real time.” It’s not surprising that Samsung wants to put more AI on its watches, but I wonder what the company has in store for the Watch 9 and Ultra 2. There’s already an AI running coach, but perhaps Galaxy AI will act more like Google Health's AI Personal Health Coach? Hopefully, this doesn’t mean we’ll need a subscription.

Samsung says the next Galaxy Watch is built with “all-new internal components,” suggesting the BioActive health sensor is being upgraded. This could point to more accurate health monitoring and fitness tracking, but it could also mean a new chipset. Samsung is expected to use Qualcomm’s latest Snapdragon Wear Elite, which will deliver faster performance and charging while enhancing on-device AI capabilities. The Watch 9 and Ultra 2 will likely be among the first to feature the new chip, so we’re excited to see how they perform!

One thing we can definitely expect from Samsung’s upcoming watches is Blood Pressure Trends. After years without access to blood pressure tracking features, Samsung has finally brought the capability to the U.S., and it’s taking things a step further by launching Blood Pressure Trends to replace Vascular Load. Blood pressure trends will “check your blood pressure periodically to show you a trend over time. It will also give you tips to help support healthy habits.”

While remembering the original Pixel Fold’s design, I wanted to focus on how apps behaved on the phone's wider aspect ratio. On the outer screen, apps were shorter and wider than on a normal Android phone. This looked funny at first, but I quickly came to appreciate it because everything was easier to access with a single hand.

Things got even better when opening the phone, as apps automatically opened in landscape tablet orientation. This is a huge deal because most apps on modern book-style foldables, like the Galaxy Z Fold 7, still open like a giant phone app rather than in a tablet format, as they should.

Google did a lot of great work in Android 12L to fix this problem, but a foldable shaped like a landscape tablet just works better, since it doesn’t require specific app-level code to get things right. I hope the Z Fold 8 works this way since it’s supposed to be a wider aspect ratio.

The one thing I’m not looking forward to with the Galaxy Z Fold 8’s smaller stature is the rumored downgrade in camera hardware. Samsung is said to be outfitting the wider Z Fold 8 with just a pair of cameras on the back. More specifically, that’s a 50MP main camera and a 50MP ultrawide camera. Not exactly great-sounding specs from a 2026 flagship.

That means no telephoto camera at all, which is one of my favorite ways to get great photos out of the Galaxy S26 Ultra. To make matters worse, a 50MP sensor isn’t exactly cutting-edge and will likely just be a rehash of the sensors Samsung has been using on its Z Fold line for years. I hope I’m pleasantly surprised by the official announcement, because this could be the one black mark on the Z Fold 8’s spec sheet.

While I’d rather have the smaller phone, Samsung is apparently souping up the bigger Z Fold 8 with more features from the Galaxy S26 Ultra and calling it the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra because of it. There’s been a mix of rumors around what Samsung’s plan for the “Ultra” moniker on this phone actually means, but the company will likely bring some of the best hardware features from the S26 Ultra.

My hope is that means the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra gets the S26 Ultra’s camera hardware. More specifically, I’d love to see the 200MP main camera and 5x telephoto ALoP camera that were the highlight of the Galaxy S26 Ultra’s camera for me. The Z Fold series has never been a camera powerhouse, but maybe this year’s Ultra entry will finally change that for good.

Samsung’s new Flex Titanium technology aims to define a new generation of truly crease-free foldables, and I can’t wait to see how it holds up over a lifetime of constant folding. That crease has been a big complaint about foldable phones for years, and it’s been fun watching companies try new things to try to eliminate it. It’s wild enough that we can fold glass in the first place, but seeing it work without a worn-down crease will be the next-most impressive thing.

Alongside the new foldables, Samsung is also expected to introduce its latest Galaxy smartwatches, including the Galaxy Watch 9 series and a new Galaxy Watch Ultra. To prepare for the launch, Samsung has begun a major visual overhaul of the Wearables app. A recent leak revealed the company is reworking the app's UI, making it much cleaner and aligning it with the new One UI aesthetic. Beyond a larger smartwatch render when you open the app, Galaxy AI will reportedly be able to generate custom info tiles for your watch, with users simply describing what they want to see. Samsung is also reportedly working on a new Vitals tool that combines five distinct health metrics to calculate and improve your Energy Score.

Shifting gears back to the foldables, there's some bad news if you're in the market for a new phone this year. Rising RAM and component prices have pushed nearly every hardware maker to increase device pricing, and it looks like Samsung won't be an exception. Rumors suggest the Galaxy Z Fold 8 will start at a steep $1,900 in the U.S., while the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (the actual successor to the Galaxy Z Fold 7) will start at $2,100. That's a $100 increase over last year's Galaxy Z Fold 7, something that might leave a sour taste in the mouths of potential buyers, but something we could very realistically see happen.

One of the major upgrades I'm looking forward to this year is the increase in the Galaxy Z Fold's battery capacity. Samsung has stuck with the same 4,400mAh cell for the past five years, but according to recent leaks, this is finally the year it bumps things up to 5,000mAh on the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra. That still won't bring it anywhere near Chinese competitors like the Oppo Find N6 or Honor Magic V6, but it should put Z Fold 8 Ultra buyers in a far more comfortable spot. I'm starting to wonder what took Samsung this long, but if we're getting the same thin design as last year with a bigger battery, I'm not complaining.

The biggest upgrade to the Galaxy Z Fold 8 series might not be the new form factor at all. In my opinion, it could be the crease-free display that debuts with the device. Just ahead of Galaxy Unpacked, Samsung officially showcased its new Flex Titanium technology. The company says it has reworked the foldable display, adding a new titanium plate beneath the OLED panel designed to significantly improve structural durability and make the screen's center crease far less visible — something that's long been an issue for Galaxy Z Fold users. Too much of a coincidence with the foldable iPhone arriving later this year? I don't think so.

Speaking of Samsung showcasing technology that'll debut on the Galaxy Z Fold 8, a recent report revealed that the company has already started displaying the phone at Samsung stores in Korea. A Reddit user reportedly visited a Samsung store where the new foldables were already on display. The showcase didn't include official device names, but the phones on show looked nearly identical to the leaked renders, giving us a real-world look at the Galaxy Z Fold 8. This might be the first instance Samsung has publicly displayed a device ahead of its own launch event.

We’ve been hearing about Samsung’s smart glasses for a while now, and we have yet to get them. What we did get in May was “Intelligent Eyewear.” This was a collaboration with Google, Gentle Monster, and Warby Parker. Now, personally speaking, this isn’t exactly what I’m looking forward to. What I’m looking forward to are Samsung’s glasses in development: codename Jinju and Haen. These are Samsung's take on the Meta Ray-Ban, and they should be quite interesting.

We don't know many details about the Galaxy Glasses yet, but it looks like Samsung is using Qualcomm's Snapdragon AR1 platform. These will take on Meta's Ray-Ban glasses, but with a camera that sits closer to the eye level. Another key piece of information suggests Gemini integration will be available out of the box, and that these glasses will rely on bone‑conduction tech to deliver sound. I can understand why Samsung would be interested in these glasses; they'd be ideal to pair with Galaxy phones, and with Meta gaining a huge amount of early ground and building on that with its own smart glasses, Samsung is looking to make its own debut with the Galaxy Glasses. It's unclear whether we'll see these glasses this week, but Samsung has been known to share early previews of its products in the past, so stay tuned.

What I want to see is Samsung's take on display glasses. The MemoMind One and Even Realities G2 showcased how waveguides can be used to good effect, and these glasses actually make a difference in daily use that goes beyond mirroring notifications. The MemoMind One records and automatically transcribes audio, and this is such a time-saving feature when attending meetings. While this tech is still in its infancy, we should see heady gains on a yearly basis — there's going to be full-color waveguides starting as early as 2027, and it will be interesting to see how those measure up. Ahead of all that though, Samsung needs to have a foot in the door.; we'll just have to wait a little longer to see what comes out of Korea.

As for what the Galaxy Glasses actually look like, an early leak suggested a design that's largely similar to the Meta Ray-Ban, with a rounded lens, camera on the left, and LED indicator on the right. The most intriguing thing about the glasses is that they run One UI XR, a derivative of Android XR designed to be used by Samsung. While this is just theorized, a leak suggested the glasses can be controlled with the Galaxy Ring, and that's exciting to me; this is a big reason why I use the Even Realities G2 and its unique control ring, and if Samsung can emulate the feature, it will make the Galaxy Glasses stand out just that little bit more. As is the case with all smart glasses, Gemini is going to take center-stage, and we should also get the new avatar of Gemini Live.

There isn’t much else to talk about Samsung’s smart glasses, but it will be interesting to see if we get a preview of what’s to come later in the year. Android XR is a bold move, and with Samsung building out One UI XR, there’s plenty of interest in seeing how the ecosystem builds out alongside Samsung’s phones, tablets, and other devices.

We’ve talked a lot about the differences in size and aspect ratio between the Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Z Fold 8 Ultra. I know which one I’d prefer, and it’s not the wider option, but I’m curious about what you think: Does the wider Galaxy Z Fold 8 or square Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra appeal to you more?

I haven’t spent much time with the Galaxy Z Fold 7, but I do think it’s quite amazing how Samsung managed to make it so thin. That said, I do wish Samsung had stuck a larger battery in it, especially since the Galaxy S26 Ultra has a larger one than Samsung’s foldables. That’s one thing I’m hoping to see change with the Galaxy Z Fold 8: more battery capacity, ideally in the form of a silicone-carbon battery, as we got with the Motorola Razr Fold.

I keep wanting to love the Galaxy Z Flip, and Samsung keeps getting close, but the cover screen experience continues to ruin it. After years of Motorola showing everyone how it’s done with the Razr, Samsung still officially supports only a handful of apps on the Galaxy Z Flip 7's cover screen, while you have to use Good Lock to enable more. It’s pretty dumb, to be honest, and there’s no reason Motorola can figure this out but Samsung can’t. With any luck, the Galaxy Z Flip 8 will finally unlock the cover screen’s full potential and fully support apps without the need to download an app and a module.

Samsung is also rumored to have a new pair of earbuds coming. It’s unclear whether they’ll be launched at Unpacked, but rumors suggest a pair of open-ear earbuds called Galaxy Buds On. Like other open-ear earbuds, they should deliver adequate sound while letting you hear both your audio and your surroundings. It’s not clear, however, whether Samsung will offer any particular advancements to stand out among other open-ear earbuds, but Galaxy fans should be happy to have yet another option from Samsung.

Switching gears to wearables, it looks like Samsung may be giving the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 massive hardware upgrades, making it truly worthy of its “Ultra” moniker. The first rumor involves a huge 36% battery capacity increase, bringing the battery up from 590mAh in the first Galaxy Watch Ultra to a massive 800mAh in the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2.

Since Ultra-types are designed to fully replace a phone while out and about, it’s important for them to have epic battery life that lasts through extra-long runs, hikes, or even long hands-free phone calls while driving. The Ultra was already the best smartwatch Samsung ever made, and this battery-size improvement will make it even better.

While we’re still on the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 topic, Samsung is rumored to upgrade the casing to an IP69K-resistant build. The company has fallen behind rivals with less rugged phone builds, but at least a more rugged watch makes plenty of sense.

IP69K takes all the great features of IP68 and IP69 water and dust resistance and enhances them with resistance against high-pressure, high-temperature jets at 80 degrees Celsius. While most people aren’t subjecting their wrists to such conditions, there’s little doubt that they go in hot tubs, use pressure washers, and do all sorts of similar activities while wearing their watch.

Lastly, the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 is said to increase its display brightness to 5,000 nits, a 67% improvement over the first-generation Galaxy Watch Ultra. A 3,000-nit display was already among the brightest on any smartwatch to date, but 5,000 nits is a pretty wild number for a little wrist-mounted display.

This number might seem like overkill at first, but anyone who has ever worn a smartwatch knows how difficult they can sometimes be to see in direct sunlight or other similar conditions. As an outdoor and fitness-focused watch, it makes a ton of sense for Samsung to push the boundaries of brightness, and it’s going to make a huge difference for anyone buying a Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 when they use it outside.

Switching back to the foldables, I was hoping this would be the phone release where Samsung finally fixes its slow display PWM dimming rate, but we haven’t seen any rumors or leaks pointing to this important fix. Samsung is the sole remaining smartphone manufacturer that hasn’t addressed the issue. While Google and Apple’s methods aren’t anywhere near as good as what’s available on most Chinese smartphones, the world’s largest tech companies need to help users facing debilitating pain from the flickering displays they’ve created.

Unlike the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra, which I got to try out extensively, I couldn't get my hands on the Galaxy Z Fold 7 for more than a day. But even that single day was more than enough to convince me that Samsung had made major changes to the Z Fold series. Even now, when I hold the Galaxy Z Fold 7 in my hand, it still feels like the best foldable form factor out there compared to its Chinese rivals, thanks to its slimness and apt cover display ratio. And with Samsung fixing the battery and the crease this year, the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra might just be peak foldable in 2026.

There's one design change Samsung isn't making, though, and I'm worried it'll continue to affect users: those flat, sharp corners. I'm not sure how Galaxy Z Fold 7 owners feel about it day-to-day, but switching from that design language to the slightly curved edges on the Galaxy S26 Ultra proved to be a huge improvement in the experience, since the phone simply doesn't dig into your palms as much. With a foldable, those sharp edges might stick out even more, especially when the phone is unfolded. I'm not sure how it'll play out in practice, but it's the first thing I'll be checking when I go hands-on with the devices.

I’ve mentioned this briefly before, but Samsung has two Galaxy Glasses: Jinju and Haen. These aren’t exactly equal. The former is said to lean more on the audio side of assistance with no screen. However, Haen is what I’m looking forward to. A micro-LED display for AR overlays, a notification light. This has me. But, if we do see anything, and I mean anything regarding Samsung’s Galaxy Glasses at Unpacked, it might be something along the lines of Jinju.

Make no mistake, pricing has been a hot topic with Samsung’s upcoming smart glasses. They would go up against Meta’s Ray-Bans and more. So, Samsung has a line to walk here to make them appealing to the average consumer. There’s a chance one of its glasses, Jinju, could sit somewhere between $379 and $499. What’s more, these glasses could feature a 12MP camera.

The Galaxy Watch 9 is where my eyes are going this summer’s Galaxy Unpacked—for good reason, too. I’ve kept my ear to the ground: the watch could keep that squircle design the Watch 8 had (that’s a good thing, personally). Samsung also teased the device recently, stating it will strive to become a “health-first, always-on gateway” for users.

Another Galaxy Watch 9 teaser that’s got me raising an eyebrow is what Samsung said about the watch: “increased battery life.” The Galaxy Watch 8 has a pretty decent battery life, don’t get me wrong. But, I’m inclined to agree with my colleagues here: it could’ve been better. Hopefully, when Samsung says it’s “increased,” they mean by more than a few minutes. Give me a few more hours, then we’re cooking.

Planting a flag on the “health-first” field means something to Samsung. It means something to me, too. Accuracy has to be on point, metrics have to be detailed, but not overwhelming. I should be able to understand at a glance what these numbers are for me and my wellbeing. Samsung has said as much in recent days, and I’m crossing my fingers that it's true for the Galaxy Watch 9.

The Galaxy Watch 9 was also touted to continue offering its 40mm and 44mm variants. I like that smaller watch face. It feels a little nicer on the wrist, and looks more aesthetically pleasing. However, rumors suggest Samsung could alter the Galaxy Watch 9’s band this year. While the Watch 8’s band was smoother, the Watch 9 could offer a dip in the center, almost like a racing stripe.

item.title: Google Pixel 11 Pro XL vs. Pixel 10 Pro XL: A glowing upgrade
item.description: Google might be reusing the same design again with the Pixel 11, but this time, it's packing an extra glow.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Sutrich
item.dc:description: <p>Nick started with DOS and NES and uses those fond memories of floppy disks and cartridges to fuel his opinions on modern tech. Because of this, he covers both smartphones and VR technology, two avenues that split his passions right down the middle. From Nokia fan to Android fanatic, Nick has been writing about and reviewing smartphones since 2011. An avid gamer and equally well-versed tech head, Nick worked in the IT industry for 15 years, helping to further develop his technical knowledge, which has become particularly important in his fight with PWM sensitivity and deep dives into display technology. He's a huge fan of any phone that can fold in half and loves getting into the nitty-gritty with folding phone coverage for the site. He's also got over a decade of experience with VR gaming, having used the original Oculus DK1 and every major VR headset since then, passionately covering Android Central's Meta Quest content and smart glasses coverage with his weekly thVRsday column on Thursdays. Beyond that, you'll find Nick taking photos of anything and everything, from the beautiful mountains of his home to the chickens in his backyard, and using them to compare cameras to help you choose the best one.</p>

media:content.media:description: A white Google Pixel 10 Pro XL next to a mockup of a Google Pixel 11 Pro XL with a Thinborne case
